rules.rule_description: For affirmative sentences with 'is/am/are/was/were', form a negative interrogative by moving the verb to the front and adding 'n't' (or 'not' after the subject).

examples.original: The bee is one of the busiest insects.
examples.converted: Is the bee one of the busiest insects?

examples.original: Optimism is a great quality.
examples.converted: Is optimism a great quality?

examples.original: It is a vivid quality.
examples.converted: Is it a vivid quality?

examples.original: The Sundarbans is one of the largest mangrove forests in the world.
examples.converted: Is the Sundarbans one of the largest mangrove forests in the world?

examples.original: The Padma is one of the mightiest rivers of Bangladesh.
examples.converted: Is the Padma one of the mightiest rivers of Bangladesh?

examples.original: Copying in the exams is one of the greatest social crimes.
examples.converted: Is copying in the exams one of the greatest social crimes?

examples.original: A freedom fighter is the greatest son of the soil.
examples.converted: Is a freedom fighter the greatest son of the soil?

examples.original: Newspaper is one of the most useful sources of knowledge.
examples.converted: Is newspaper one of the most useful sources of knowledge?

examples.original: It is called the people’s parliament.
examples.converted: Is it called the people’s parliament?

examples.original: Simplicity is a great virtue.
examples.converted: Is simplicity a great virtue?

examples.original: It is one of the best qualities.
examples.converted: Is it one of the best qualities?

examples.original: A simple man is one of the happiest men.
examples.converted: Is a simple man one of the happiest men?

examples.original: Jackfruit is one of the largest fruits of Bangladesh.
examples.converted: Is jackfruit one of the largest fruits of Bangladesh?

examples.original: The student is known to me.
examples.converted: Is the student known to me?

examples.original: Bangladesh is a low-lying country.
examples.converted: Is Bangladesh a low-lying country?

examples.original: Computer is one of the greatest inventions of modern science.
examples.converted: Is computer one of the greatest inventions of modern science?

examples.original: Water is a liquid substance.
examples.converted: Is water a liquid substance?

examples.original: Pure water is one of the most valuable things for our lives.
examples.converted: Is pure water one of the most valuable things for our lives?

examples.original: A successful man is an asset to a country.
examples.converted: Is a successful man an asset to a country?

examples.original: Honesty is the best way of leading life.
examples.converted: Is honesty the best way of leading life?

examples.original: Man is the best creation of God.
examples.converted: Is man the best creation of God?

examples.original: Patriotism is a noble virtue.
examples.converted: Is patriotism a noble virtue?

examples.original: William Wordsworth is one of the best well-known poets in English literature.
examples.converted: Is William Wordsworth one of the best well-known poets in English literature?

examples.original: He was one of the greatest dramatists in Bangla literature.
examples.converted: Was he one of the greatest dramatists in Bangla literature?

examples.original: He was an ardent follower of Lord Byron.
examples.converted: Was he an ardent follower of Lord Byron?

examples.original: Mother Teresa is an icon to many people.
examples.converted: Is Mother Teresa an icon to many people?

examples.original: She was one of the greatest persons of the world.
examples.converted: Was she one of the greatest persons of the world?

examples.original: A flower is a glowing gift of nature.
examples.converted: Is a flower a glowing gift of nature?

examples.original: The rose is the best of all flowers.
examples.converted: Is the rose the best of all flowers?

examples.original: Water is one of the most useful elements in nature.
examples.converted: Is water one of the most useful elements in nature?

examples.original: Train is one of the most comfortable vehicles.
examples.converted: Is train one of the most comfortable vehicles?

examples.original: It was introduced by the British govt.
examples.converted: Was it introduced by the British govt.?

examples.original: The moon is the only sub-planet of the earth.
examples.converted: Is the moon the only sub-planet of the earth?

examples.original: The moon is the most beautiful thing.
examples.converted: Is the moon the most beautiful thing?

examples.original: He was a great patriot.
examples.converted: Was he a great patriot?

rules.rule_description: For affirmative sentences in the present or past indefinite tense, use the negative auxiliaries 'don't/doesn't/didn't' before the subject.

examples.original: Everybody wants to make a good result in the examination.
examples.converted: Does everybody want to make a good result in the examination?

examples.original: People of all ages enjoy a moonlit night.
examples.converted: Do people of all ages enjoy a moonlit night?

examples.original: Everybody likes a moonlit night.
examples.converted: Does everybody like a moonlit night?

examples.original: Everybody knows this.
examples.converted: Does everybody know this?

examples.original: Everyone likes the Hilsa fish of the river.
examples.converted: Does everyone like the Hilsa fish of the river?

examples.original: Everybody appreciates this quality.
examples.converted: Does everybody appreciate this quality?

examples.original: Everybody uses it.
examples.converted: Does everybody use it?

examples.original: Almost everybody knows the importance of honesty.
examples.converted: Does almost everybody know the importance of honesty?

examples.original: People of all ages enjoy this game.
examples.converted: Do people of all ages enjoy this game?

examples.original: He inherited vast property from his father and sister.
examples.converted: Did he inherit vast property from his father and sister?

examples.original: Everybody respects her.
examples.converted: Does everybody respect her?

examples.original: All the students respect him.
examples.converted: Do all the students respect him?

examples.original: People of all ages like to watch television.
examples.converted: Do people of all ages like to watch television?

examples.original: Most of the people here live in the villages.
examples.converted: Do most of the people here live in the villages?

examples.original: English opens a door to knowledge.
examples.converted: Does English open a door to knowledge?

examples.original: Every man loves reading interesting stories.
examples.converted: Does every man love reading interesting stories?

examples.original: People suffer a lot from waterlogging.
examples.converted: Do people suffer a lot from waterlogging?

rules.rule_description: For sentences with modal auxiliaries (can, could, should, must), move the modal to the front and make it negative (can't, shouldn't, etc.).

examples.original: We can attract the attention of others when we are optimistic.
examples.converted: Can we attract the attention of others when we are optimistic?

examples.original: Women should work as much as men.
examples.converted: Should women work as much as men?

examples.original: A man must enjoy a train journey.
examples.converted: Must a man enjoy a train journey?

examples.original: We should be optimistic about life.
examples.converted: Should we be optimistic about life?

examples.original: You must learn English.
examples.converted: Must you learn English?

examples.original: Students should observe the rules of health.
examples.converted: Should students observe the rules of health?

examples.original: We should be patriots.
examples.converted: Should we be patriots?

examples.original: We should read books to gain knowledge.
examples.converted: Should we read books to gain knowledge?

examples.original: We should read newspaper to know the outside world more.
examples.converted: Should we read newspaper to know the outside world more?

examples.original: A student can visit all the renowned libraries of the world without going there.
examples.converted: Can a student visit all the renowned libraries of the world without going there?

examples.original: He can succeed in life.
examples.converted: Can he succeed in life?

examples.original: The government has taken necessary steps to keep the price hike under control.
examples.converted: Has the government taken necessary steps to keep the price hike under control?

examples.original: The culprits should be punished.
examples.converted: Should the culprits be punished?

examples.original: Illiterate people should be given opportunities.
examples.converted: Should illiterate people be given opportunities?

rules.rule_description: For sentences with 'there is/are/was/were', invert 'there' and the verb.

examples.original: There is no student without a smart phone.
examples.converted: Is there no student without a smart phone?

examples.original: There was an old man in a village.
examples.converted: Was there an old man in a village?

examples.original: There are two umpires who conduct the game.
examples.converted: Are there two umpires who conduct the game?

rules.rule_description: For sentences with 'nobody/no one/none', change to 'anybody/anyone' and use an auxiliary verb.

examples.original: Nobody respects him.
examples.converted: Does anybody respect him?

examples.original: Nobody wants his children to be terrorists in the future.
examples.converted: Does anybody want his children to be terrorists in the future?

examples.original: No one can deny its necessity.
examples.converted: Can anyone deny its necessity?

rules.rule_description: For imperative 'Let us', use 'shall we'.

examples.original: Let us drink tube-well water.
examples.converted: Shall we drink tube-well water?

examples.original: Let us use it in our everyday life.
examples.converted: Shall we use it in our everyday life?

examples.original: Let us lead a simple life.
examples.converted: Shall we lead a simple life?

rules.rule_description: Add 'not' after auxiliary verb; change 'some' to 'any', 'already' to 'yet'.

examples.original: It is a very important asset.
examples.converted: It is not a very important asset.

examples.original: It is a great curse.
examples.converted: It is not a great curse.

examples.original: The river is very useful to our farmers.
examples.converted: The river is not very useful to our farmers.

examples.original: Our examination system should be moderated.
examples.converted: Our examination system should not be moderated.

examples.original: The government is sincere in removing this crime.
examples.converted: The government is not sincere in removing this crime.

examples.original: It contains authentic news.
examples.converted: It does not contain authentic news.

examples.original: A journey by train is always enjoyable.
examples.converted: A journey by train is not always enjoyable.

examples.original: Initially the questions may seem difficult.
examples.converted: Initially the questions may not seem difficult.

examples.original: Waterlogging is a very painful problem in Dhaka city.
examples.converted: Waterlogging is not a very painful problem in Dhaka city.

examples.original: Illiteracy is a major problem in society.
examples.converted: Illiteracy is not a major problem in society.

examples.original: Reading books is a source of great pleasure.
examples.converted: Reading books is not a source of great pleasure.

rules.rule_description: For 'only + noun/person', change to 'None but' (for persons) or 'Nothing but' (for things).

examples.original: Only the optimistic people are accepted by all.
examples.converted: None but the optimistic people are accepted by all.

examples.original: Only a hard working person can see the light of prosperity.
examples.converted: None but a hard working person can see the light of prosperity.

examples.original: Only the foolish can disobey the true importance of English.
examples.converted: None but the foolish can disobey the true importance of English.

examples.original: Only the moon shines at night.
examples.converted: None but the moon shines at night.

examples.original: Only truth lasts long.
examples.converted: None but truth lasts long.

examples.original: Only perseverance of the young people can solve this problem.
examples.converted: None but perseverance of the young people can solve this problem.

examples.original: Only you can do the work.
examples.converted: None but you can do the work.

rules.rule_description: For 'must', change to 'cannot but' or 'cannot help'.

examples.original: We must learn how to use computers.
examples.converted: We cannot but learn how to use computers.

examples.original: We must try our best to improve its present position.
examples.converted: We cannot but try our best to improve its present position.

examples.original: You must learn English.
examples.converted: You cannot but learn English.

rules.rule_description: Change superlative or comparative affirmative degrees into positive negative degrees.

examples.original: The earth is bigger than the moon.
examples.converted: The moon is not as big as the earth.

examples.original: A corrupt person is the most hated person.
examples.converted: No other person is as hated as a corrupt person.

examples.original: The bee is the most industrious insect.
examples.converted: No insect is as industrious as the bee.

examples.original: War is the most corrosive thing.
examples.converted: Nothing is as corrosive as war.

examples.original: Dhaka is the most populous city in Bangladesh.
examples.converted: No other city in Bangladesh is as populous as Dhaka.

examples.original: A simple man is the happiest person among us.
examples.converted: No other person among us is as happy as a simple man.

examples.original: A failed student is the worst student in the class.
examples.converted: No other student in the class is as bad as a failed student.

examples.original: Bangladesh is one of the most populous countries in the world.
examples.converted: Very few countries in the world are as populous as Bangladesh.

examples.original: Illiteracy is one of the most severe problems.
examples.converted: Very few problems are as severe as illiteracy.

rules.rule_description: For 'both...and', change to 'not only... but also' to preserve meaning while introducing a negative modifier.

examples.original: They give us both knowledge and pleasure.
examples.converted: They give us not only knowledge but also pleasure.

rules.rule_description: For 'too...to', change to 'so...that...cannot'.

examples.original: He was too tired to walk.
examples.converted: He was so tired that he could not walk.

examples.original: Reading is too important for us to ignore.
examples.converted: Reading is so important that we cannot ignore it.

rules.rule_description: For 'as soon as', change to 'No sooner had...than'.

examples.original: As soon as I saw the tiger, I climbed upon the tree.
examples.converted: No sooner had I seen the tiger than I climbed upon the tree.

examples.original: As soon as their real identity gets revealed, people leave them.
examples.converted: No sooner does their real identity get revealed than people leave them.

rules.rule_description: For sentences with 'very' + adjective, use 'How + adjective + subject + verb!'

examples.original: It is a very turbulent river.
examples.converted: How turbulent the river is!

examples.original: The river is very useful to our farmers.
examples.converted: How useful the river is to our farmers!

examples.original: It is a very serious crime.
examples.converted: How serious a crime it is!

examples.original: Flower cultivation is very profitable now.
examples.converted: How profitable flower cultivation is now!

examples.original: Flower cultivators are very industrious.
examples.converted: How industrious flower cultivators are!

examples.original: Laurel is very beautiful.
examples.converted: How beautiful laurel is!

examples.original: The rose is a very nice flower.
examples.converted: How nice the rose is!

examples.original: A train journey is very safe.
examples.converted: How safe a train journey is!

examples.original: The beauty of a moonlit night is very charming.
examples.converted: How charming the beauty of a moonlit night is!

examples.original: The recent flood was very devastating.
examples.converted: How devastating the recent flood was!

examples.original: Flood is a very dangerous disaster.
examples.converted: How dangerous a disaster flood is!

examples.original: Computer is nowadays a very useful tool.
examples.converted: How useful a tool computer is nowadays!

examples.original: The food was very hot.
examples.converted: How hot the food was!

examples.original: I was very excited to see the seabeach.
examples.converted: How excited I was to see the seabeach!

examples.original: It is a very beautiful seabeach.
examples.converted: How beautiful a seabeach it is!

examples.original: A successful man is very happy.
examples.converted: How happy a successful man is!

examples.original: Television is a very wonderful invention of modern science.
examples.converted: How wonderful an invention television is!

examples.original: Some books are very interesting.
examples.converted: How interesting some books are!

rules.rule_description: For emphasis with 'great' or noun phrase, use 'What + noun phrase + subject + verb!'

examples.original: It is a great curse.
examples.converted: What a great curse it is!

examples.original: He becomes a very valuable person.
examples.converted: What a very valuable person he becomes!

examples.original: It is a very important asset.
examples.converted: What a very important asset it is!

examples.original: It is a vivid quality.
examples.converted: What a vivid quality it is!

transformation_categories.category_name: Simple to Compound and Complex to Compound

rules.rule_description: Change simple sentence with infinitive of purpose to compound using 'and'.

examples.original: Bees store honey in the hive to eat during winter.
examples.converted: Bees store honey in the hive, and they eat it during winter.

examples.original: We need flowers to decorate a place.
examples.converted: We want to decorate a place, and so we need flowers.

examples.original: He disguised himself as a poor traveller to survive.
examples.converted: He wanted to survive, and so he disguised himself as a poor traveller.

examples.original: We should read books to acquire knowledge.
examples.converted: We should read books, and we will acquire knowledge.

examples.original: He contributed much money to education to educate poor students.
examples.converted: He contributed much money to education, and he educated poor students.

examples.original: We should take regular physical exercise and eat a balanced diet to improve our health.
examples.converted: We should take regular physical exercise and eat a balanced diet, and this will improve our health.

rules.rule_description: Change complex sentence with 'though/although' to compound using 'but'.

examples.original: Though we have strict laws, we are still affected by this evil.
examples.converted: We have strict laws, but we are still affected by this evil.

examples.original: Though the moon gives us light, it does not have its own light.
examples.converted: The moon gives us light, but it does not have its own light.

examples.original: Though he had vast wealth, he didn’t spend it on himself.
examples.converted: He had vast wealth, but he didn’t spend it on himself.

examples.original: Though they are cowards, they are clever.
examples.converted: They are cowards, but they are clever.

examples.original: Though there are laws to punish terrorists, they are not properly applied.
examples.converted: There are laws to punish terrorists, but they are not properly applied.

examples.original: Though it is a blessing, it has its dark sides.
examples.converted: It is a blessing, but it has its dark sides.

examples.original: Though we take the utmost care, we cannot avoid death.
examples.converted: We take the utmost care, but we cannot avoid death.

examples.original: Though a healthy man is an asset to his family, an unhealthy man is a liability.
examples.converted: A healthy man is an asset to his family, but an unhealthy man is a liability.

examples.original: Though cricket is a costly game, people of all classes enjoy playing it.
examples.converted: Cricket is a costly game, but people of all classes enjoy playing it.

rules.rule_description: Change simple sentence with participle phrase to compound using 'and'.

examples.original: Seeing the bees, we think of the importance of activity.
examples.converted: We see the bees, and we think of the importance of activity.

examples.original: Collecting subscriptions, they bought relief goods for the flood-affected people.
examples.converted: They collected subscriptions, and they bought relief goods for the flood-affected people.

examples.original: Entering the kingdom of the prince, he captured a large village.
examples.converted: He entered the kingdom of the prince, and he captured a large village.

examples.original: Going to the Sundarbans, we saw many animals.
examples.converted: We went to the Sundarbans, and we saw many animals.

examples.original: By working hard, we can improve our lot.
examples.converted: We must work hard, and then we can improve our lot.

examples.original: By reading good books, we can enrich our minds.
examples.converted: We read good books, and we can enrich our minds.

examples.original: By paying taxes, he obeys the law.
examples.converted: He pays his taxes, and he obeys the law.

examples.original: By using it, we can get many new pieces of information.
examples.converted: We use it, and we can get many new pieces of information.

examples.original: By applying the law properly, we can get rid of it.
examples.converted: We apply the law properly, and we can get rid of it.

rules.rule_description: Change complex sentence with 'if' (conditional) to compound using 'and' or 'or'.

examples.original: If you study well, you will be able to learn it.
examples.converted: Study well, and you will be able to learn it.

examples.original: If you read, you will learn.
examples.converted: Read, and you will learn.

examples.original: If we destroy mangrove forests, we will lose our ecological balance.
examples.converted: Destroy mangrove forests, and we will lose our ecological balance.

examples.original: If we resist this malady, we can strengthen our education.
examples.converted: Resist this malady, and we can strengthen our education.

examples.original: If we use time properly, we will benefit.
examples.converted: Use time properly, and we will benefit.

examples.original: If you answer all the questions correctly, you will get good marks.
examples.converted: Answer all the questions correctly, and you will get good marks.

examples.original: If you watch television, you can learn many things.
examples.converted: Watch television, and you can learn many things.

examples.original: If we ensure education for all, our society will progress.
examples.converted: Ensure education for all, and our society will progress.

rules.rule_description: Change simple sentence with 'in spite of/despite' to compound using 'but/yet'.

examples.original: In spite of his poverty, he is honest.
examples.converted: He is poor, but he is honest.

examples.original: Despite working hard, she failed the exam.
examples.converted: She worked hard, yet she failed the exam.

examples.original: In spite of being sick, he attended the meeting.
examples.converted: He was sick, but he attended the meeting.

examples.original: Despite his enormous wealth, he is unhappy.
examples.converted: He has enormous wealth, yet he is unhappy.

rules.rule_description: Change complex sentence with 'as/since/when' to compound using 'and/and so'.

examples.original: As the weather was rough, we stayed home.
examples.converted: The weather was rough, and so we stayed home.

examples.original: Since he is weak, he cannot walk fast.
examples.converted: He is weak, and therefore he cannot walk fast.

examples.original: When the teacher entered the classroom, the students stood up.
examples.converted: The teacher entered the classroom, and the students stood up.

examples.original: Since the water is salty, they cannot drink it.
examples.converted: The water is salty, and so they cannot drink it.

rules.rule_description: Change simple sentence with 'besides + gerund' to compound using 'not only... but also'.

examples.original: Besides being a brilliant student, she is a fine singer.
examples.converted: She is not only a brilliant student but also a fine singer.

examples.original: Besides giving me advice, he helped me with money.
examples.converted: He not only gave me advice but also helped me with money.

examples.original: Besides working in the office, he manages his family business.
examples.converted: He not only works in the office but also manages his family business.

rules.rule_description: Change simple sentence with participle phrase to complex by adding subordinate clause (when/if/as).

examples.original: Seeing the bees, we think of the importance of activity.
examples.converted: When we see the bees, we think of the importance of activity.

examples.original: Being industrious, everyone can prosper in life.
examples.converted: If everyone is industrious, they can prosper.

examples.original: By working hard, we can improve our lot.
examples.converted: If we work hard, we can improve our lot.

examples.original: By visiting the forest, we can enjoy beautiful sights.
examples.converted: If we visit the forest, we can enjoy beautiful sights.

examples.original: By following the process, every student can achieve a good result.
examples.converted: If every student follows the process, he can achieve a good result.

examples.original: By learning computer well, we can build our career.
examples.converted: If we learn computer well, we can build our career.

examples.original: By taking proper steps, we can improve their condition.
examples.converted: If we take proper steps, we can improve their condition.

examples.original: Having sweet scent and beauty, we love it very much.
examples.converted: As it has sweet scent and beauty, we love it very much.

examples.original: Entering the kingdom of the prince, he captured a large village.
examples.converted: When he entered the kingdom of the prince, he captured a large village.

examples.original: Seeing Muhsin, the thief began to cry.
examples.converted: When the thief saw Muhsin, he began to cry.

examples.original: Collecting subscription, they bought relief goods.
examples.converted: After they collected subscription, they bought relief goods.

examples.original: Going to the Sunderbans, we saw many animals.
examples.converted: When we went to the Sunderbans, we saw many animals.

rules.rule_description: Change simple sentence with infinitive of purpose to complex using 'so that'.

examples.original: We should read books to acquire knowledge.
examples.converted: We should read books so that we can acquire knowledge.

examples.original: Farmers work hard to support themselves.
examples.converted: Farmers work hard so that they can support themselves.

examples.original: A student should try to answer all the questions to do well.
examples.converted: A student should try to answer all the questions so that he can do well.

examples.original: We need flowers to decorate a place.
examples.converted: We need flowers so that we can decorate a place.

examples.original: He disguised himself as a poor traveller to survive.
examples.converted: He disguised himself as a poor traveller so that he could survive.

examples.original: She worked hard to shine in life.
examples.converted: She worked hard so that she could shine in life.

examples.original: We should eat the fruit to ensure nutrition.
examples.converted: We should eat the fruit so that we can ensure nutrition.

rules.rule_description: Change simple sentence with adjective phrase to complex using 'who/which/that'.

examples.original: A man having patriotic zeal is called a patriot.
examples.converted: A man who has patriotic zeal is called a patriot.

examples.original: People living below the poverty line lead a miserable life.
examples.converted: People who live below the poverty line lead a miserable life.

examples.original: The programmes telecast by television are very interesting.
examples.converted: The programmes that are telecast by television are very interesting.

examples.original: A student copying in the exam only deceives himself.
examples.converted: A student who copies in the exam only deceives himself.

examples.original: He had a basket full of caps.
examples.converted: He had a basket which was full of caps.

examples.original: There lived an old woman in the house.
examples.converted: There lived an old woman who was in the house.

examples.original: He was born at Sagordari on the bank of the Kapotaksho.
examples.converted: He was born at Sagordari which is on the bank of the Kapotaksho.

examples.original: His teachers recognized him as a precious child.
examples.converted: His teachers recognized him as a child who was precious.

examples.original: The students studying regularly can expect a good result.
examples.converted: The students who study regularly can expect a good result.

rules.rule_description: Change simple sentence with 'without' to complex using 'if not'.

examples.original: Without learning English we cannot hope to get any good job.
examples.converted: If we do not learn English, we cannot hope to get any good job.

examples.original: Without following this process, you cannot bring a good result.
examples.converted: If you do not follow this process, you cannot bring a good result.

examples.original: Without regular practice, reading skills will not improve.
examples.converted: If you do not practice regularly, reading skills will not improve.

examples.original: Without computer, we cannot imagine our modern life.
examples.converted: If we do not have a computer, we cannot imagine our modern life.

rules.rule_description: For 'who does not' (negative interrogative), change to 'everybody/everyone' in the affirmative.

examples.original: Who does not want to succeed in life?
examples.converted: Everybody wants to succeed in life.

examples.original: Who doesn't know this truth?
examples.converted: Everybody knows this truth.

examples.original: Who does not know the consequences of a tsunami?
examples.converted: Everybody knows the consequences of a tsunami.

examples.original: Who does not know of his kindness?
examples.converted: Everybody knows of his kindness.

examples.original: Who does not know about the charity of Mother Teresa?
examples.converted: Everybody knows about the charity of Mother Teresa.

examples.original: Who doesn't like the moon?
examples.converted: Everybody likes the moon.

examples.original: Who doesn't know about the rose?
examples.converted: Everybody knows about the rose.

examples.original: Who is not currently under the threat of violence?
examples.converted: Everybody is currently under the threat of violence.

examples.original: Who does not like social networking?
examples.converted: Everybody likes social networking.

rules.rule_description: For 'who' followed by an affirmative verb, change to 'nobody/no one/none' in the negative.

examples.original: Who can avoid death?
examples.converted: Nobody can avoid death.

examples.original: Who believes a liar?
examples.converted: Nobody believes a liar.

examples.original: Who cares about such trivial matters?
examples.converted: No one cares about such trivial matters.

examples.original: Who has ever seen the wind?
examples.converted: No one has ever seen the wind.

rules.rule_description: For general negative interrogative questions with auxiliary verbs ('isn't/wasn't/don't/shouldn't'), change to a standard affirmative statement.

examples.original: Wasn’t it one of the most devastating floods in our country?
examples.converted: It was one of the most devastating floods in our country.

examples.original: Shouldn’t a man learn to live in peace?
examples.converted: A man should learn to live in peace.

examples.original: Isn’t it our responsibility to do good deeds?
examples.converted: It is our responsibility to do good deeds.

examples.original: Don’t books introduce us to the realm of knowledge?
examples.converted: Books introduce us to the realm of knowledge.

examples.original: Shouldn’t we develop the habit of reading books?
examples.converted: We should develop the habit of reading books.

examples.original: Isn't it the symbol of love and beauty?
examples.converted: It is the symbol of love and beauty.

examples.original: Don't people use flowers on different occasions?
examples.converted: People use flowers on different occasions.

examples.original: Shouldn't television telecast educative programmes?
examples.converted: Television should telecast educative programmes.

examples.original: Isn’t the price hike one of the biggest problems for them?
examples.converted: The price hike is one of the biggest problems for them.

examples.original: Wasn’t Muhsin one of the best philanthropists in the world?
examples.converted: Muhsin was one of the best philanthropists in the world.

examples.original: Don’t the crows raise the baby cuckoos?
examples.converted: The crows raise the baby cuckoos.

examples.original: Isn’t the day celebrated as Bangladesh’s Second Independence Day?
examples.converted: The day is celebrated as Bangladesh’s Second Independence Day.

rules.rule_description: For 'can't' in a negative question, change to 'can' in the affirmative statement.

examples.original: Can’t girls work as hard as boys?
examples.converted: Girls can work as hard as boys.

examples.original: Can't we solve this problem together?
examples.converted: We can solve this problem together.

examples.original: Can't the government take steps against corruption?
examples.converted: The government can take steps against corruption.

rules.rule_description: For questions beginning with 'Is there any...', change to 'There is no...' in the assertive.

examples.original: Is there any use for this broken chair?
examples.converted: There is no use for this broken chair.

examples.original: Is there any doubt about his honesty?
examples.converted: There is no doubt about his honesty.

examples.original: Is there any room for compromise?
examples.converted: There is no room for compromise.

rules.rule_description: For rhetorical questions beginning with 'What does it matter...', change to 'It does not matter...'.

examples.original: What does it matter if we lose the game?
examples.converted: It does not matter if we lose the game.

examples.original: What does it matter if he disagrees with us?
examples.converted: It does not matter if he disagrees with us.

rules.rule_description: For 'How + adjective/adverb' exclamatory, change to 'subject + verb + very + adjective/adverb'.

examples.original: How greatly they inspire us to work hard!
examples.converted: They inspire us greatly to work hard.

examples.original: How charming a moonlit night is!
examples.converted: A moonlit night is very charming.

examples.original: How nice the forest is!
examples.converted: The forest is very nice.

examples.original: How bright the student is!
examples.converted: The student is very bright.

examples.original: How attentive she was to her studies!
examples.converted: She was very attentive to her studies.

examples.original: How beautiful the rose is!
examples.converted: The rose is very beautiful.

examples.original: How interesting reading books is!
examples.converted: Reading books is very interesting.

examples.original: How useful television is!
examples.converted: Television is very useful.

examples.original: How easy it is to communicate across national borders by learning English!
examples.converted: It is very easy to communicate across national borders by learning English.

examples.original: How enriching it is to read a good book!
examples.converted: It is very enriching to read a good book.

examples.original: How happy I am now!
examples.converted: I am very happy now.

examples.original: How needy the thief was!
examples.converted: The thief was very needy.

examples.original: How devastating the war is!
examples.converted: The war is very devastating.

examples.original: How irritated the examiners become to see such irrelevant answers!
examples.converted: The examiners become very irritated to see such irrelevant answers.

examples.original: How beautiful jackfruits look in the trees!
examples.converted: Jackfruits look very beautiful in the trees.

examples.original: How miserable the life of an idle man is!
examples.converted: The life of an idle man is very miserable.

rules.rule_description: For 'What + noun phrase' exclamatory, change to 'subject + verb + a very + noun'.

examples.original: What a shameful practice it is!
examples.converted: It is a very shameful practice.

examples.original: What a beautiful piece of work the National Memorial is!
examples.converted: The National Memorial is a very beautiful piece of work.

examples.original: What an exciting scene the full-moon presents!
examples.converted: The full-moon presents a very exciting scene.

examples.original: What an outstanding quality it is!
examples.converted: It is an outstanding quality.

examples.original: What a miserable life a terrorist leads!
examples.converted: A terrorist leads a very miserable life.

examples.original: What a powerful trait truthfulness is!
examples.converted: Truthfulness is a very powerful trait.

examples.original: What an amazing milestone it is in the modern world of communication!
examples.converted: It is an amazing milestone in the modern world of communication.

examples.original: What a miserable life we lead in the rainy season!
examples.converted: We lead a very miserable life in the rainy season.

examples.original: What a bad problem it is for the city dwellers!
examples.converted: It is a very bad problem for the city dwellers.

rules.rule_description: Change complex sentence with 'when/while/as' to simple using participle phrase.

examples.original: When a student gets the question paper, he should read it attentively.
examples.converted: Getting the question paper, a student should read it attentively.

examples.original: When we read good books, we discover new worlds.
examples.converted: Reading good books, we discover new worlds.

examples.original: When I reached there, my friend received me cordially.
examples.converted: Reaching there, I was received cordially by my friend.

examples.original: When he was a boy, he desired to be an Englishman.
examples.converted: As a boy, he desired to be an Englishman.

examples.original: When he gets the question paper, he should read it carefully.
examples.converted: Getting the question paper, he should read it carefully.

examples.original: When he was eight years old, his mother died.
examples.converted: At the age of eight, his mother died.

examples.original: When the babies grow up they fly away.
examples.converted: Growing up, the babies fly away.

examples.original: When we read regularly, we develop critical thinking skills.
examples.converted: Reading regularly, we develop critical thinking skills.

rules.rule_description: Change complex sentence with 'if' to simple using 'by + gerund' or 'without'.

examples.original: If we do not cultivate the habit of speaking the truth, we cannot command the confidence of others.
examples.converted: Without cultivating the habit of speaking the truth, we cannot command the confidence of others.

examples.original: If a student answers all the questions correctly, he will get good marks.
examples.converted: By answering all the questions correctly, a student will get good marks.

examples.original: If you watch television, you can learn many things.
examples.converted: By watching television, you can learn many things.

examples.original: If we ensure education for all, our society will progress.
examples.converted: By ensuring education for all, our society will progress.

examples.original: If we read newspapers, we can enrich our knowledge.
examples.converted: By reading newspapers, we can enrich our knowledge.

examples.original: If we use time properly, we can be benefited.
examples.converted: By using time properly, we can be benefited.

examples.original: If a student fails in the examination, he suffers from inferiority complex.
examples.converted: By failing in the examination, a student suffers from inferiority complex.

rules.rule_description: Change complex sentence with 'who/which/that' to simple using adjective phrase or participle.

examples.original: The man who knows English well can go abroad.
examples.converted: The man knowing English well can go abroad.

examples.original: People who live in villages are mainly farmers.
examples.converted: People living in villages are mainly farmers.

examples.original: The people who are greedy are responsible for price spiral.
examples.converted: The greedy people are responsible for the price spiral.

examples.original: The people who live in the slum suffer terribly.
examples.converted: The people living in the slum suffer terribly.

examples.original: The education that he learnt from this institution played a better role in his life.
examples.converted: The education learnt from this institution played a better role in his life.

examples.original: Those who do not read books keep themselves aloof.
examples.converted: Non-readers keep themselves aloof.

examples.original: People who are good at computers are in great demand.
examples.converted: People good at computers are in great demand.

examples.original: The freedom fighters who died in the Liberation War are called martyrs.
examples.converted: The freedom fighters dying in the Liberation War are called martyrs.

rules.rule_description: Change complex sentence with 'so that' to simple using 'to + infinitive'.

examples.original: We must work hard so that we can earn money.
examples.converted: We must work hard to earn money.

examples.original: They must work hard so that they can prosper in life.
examples.converted: They must work hard to prosper in life.

examples.original: We should learn it so that we can get good jobs.
examples.converted: We should learn it to get good jobs.

examples.original: This quality highly motivates a man so that he can sacrifice his life for the country.
examples.converted: This quality highly motivates a man to sacrifice his life for the country.

examples.original: We have to create social awareness so that we can fight against corruption.
examples.converted: We have to create social awareness to fight against corruption.

rules.rule_description: Change compound sentence with 'and' connecting two verbs to simple using a participle.

examples.original: Bees collect nectar from the flowers and make honey.
examples.converted: Collecting nectar from the flowers, bees make honey.

examples.original: He came to a house and asked for something to eat.
examples.converted: Coming to a house, he asked for something to eat.

examples.original: He sat under the tree and fell asleep.
examples.converted: Sitting under the tree, he fell asleep.

examples.original: He went to Europe and started composing poems.
examples.converted: Going to Europe, he started composing poems.

examples.original: She set up "Nirmal Hridoy" at Kolkata and helped the poor.
examples.converted: Setting up "Nirmal Hridoy" at Kolkata, she helped the poor.

examples.original: They fought bravely and snatched the red sun of independence.
examples.converted: Fighting bravely, they snatched the red sun of independence.

examples.original: We read books and enrich our minds.
examples.converted: Reading books, we enrich our minds.

rules.rule_description: Change compound sentence with 'and' (sequential) to simple using 'after + gerund' or 'having + past participle'.

examples.original: He collected his things and went to live by himself.
examples.converted: Having collected his things, he went to live by himself.

examples.original: He did a great job and was praised highly.
examples.converted: Having done a great job, he was praised highly.

rules.rule_description: For imperative with 'Let us' or 'Let's', change to 'We should'.

examples.original: Let's be industrious like the bee.
examples.converted: We should be industrious like the bee.

examples.original: Let us not elaborate any answer unnecessarily.
examples.converted: We should not elaborate any answer unnecessarily.

examples.original: Let us all work together to make Bangladesh a corruption-free country.
examples.converted: We should all work together to make Bangladesh a corruption-free country.

examples.original: Let us lead a simple life.
examples.converted: We should lead a simple life.

examples.original: Let us be industrious to prosper in life.
examples.converted: We should be industrious to prosper in life.

examples.original: Let us save this useful river.
examples.converted: We should save this useful river.

examples.original: Let us use it in our everyday life.
examples.converted: We should use it in our everyday life.

examples.original: Let us do good deeds as our responsibility.
examples.converted: We should do good deeds as our responsibility.

examples.original: Let us answer the questions properly.
examples.converted: We should answer the questions properly.

rules.rule_description: For imperative without 'Let us', change to 'You should'.

examples.original: Take care of your health.
examples.converted: You should take care of your health.
